answer:
Definition

Jest Unit Tests solve the regression confidence problem. Without unit tests, every code change is a gamble — you don't know if fixing one bug broke three others. Unit tests verify that individual functions and modules work correctly in isolation, providing instant feedback when something breaks.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave balance calculation logic has 8 business rules (no weekends, no backdating, overlap detection, max consecutive days, etc.). Each rule has a dedicated unit test. When Manoj added a new rule ("no leave on company holidays"), the existing tests immediately caught that it accidentally broke the weekend exclusion logic — saving hours of manual testing.

Problems Solved

  • Regression prevention: Catch bugs introduced by new changes before they reach production
  • Documentation: Tests describe what the code should do — readable by new developers
  • Refactoring confidence: Change implementation freely as long as tests pass
  • Debugging speed: When a test fails, you know exactly which function broke and what the expected behavior was

Visual Guide

Code Example

// leaveCalculator.test.ts
import { calculateBusinessDays, validateLeaveRequest } from './leaveCalculator';

describe('calculateBusinessDays', () => {
  it('excludes weekends from day count', () => {
    // Monday to Friday = 5 business days
    const result = calculateBusinessDays(
      new Date('2025-03-03'), // Monday
      new Date('2025-03-07')  // Friday
    );
    expect(result).toBe(5);
  });

  it('handles spans across weekends', () => {
    // Monday to next Monday = 6 business days (skip Sat+Sun)
    const result = calculateBusinessDays(
      new Date('2025-03-03'),
      new Date('2025-03-10')
    );
    expect(result).toBe(6);
  });
});

describe('validateLeaveRequest', () => {
  it('rejects backdated leave requests', () => {
    const result = validateLeaveRequest({
      startDate: new Date('2024-01-01'), // past date
      endDate: new Date('2024-01-05'),
      balance: 10
    });
    expect(result.valid).toBe(false);
    expect(result.errors).toContain('Cannot backdate leave');
  });

  it('rejects when insufficient balance', () => {
    const result = validateLeaveRequest({
      startDate: new Date('2025-06-01'),
      endDate: new Date('2025-06-20'),
      balance: 5
    });
    expect(result.valid).toBe(false);
    expect(result.errors).toContain('Insufficient balance');
  });
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Testing implementation details instead of behavior. Tests that check internal state or private method calls break when you refactor, even though the behavior is unchanged.

Mitigation: Test the public API of your module — given inputs, assert outputs. Don't test HOW it works, test WHAT it does. If you refactor internals, tests should still pass.

Top 3 Jest Unit Test Mistakes

Mistake 1: Testing Implementation Instead of Behavior

Writing tests that are tightly coupled to internal implementation — they break on any refactor.

// ❌ BAD: Tests internal implementation
it('calls calculateDays with correct params', () => {
  const spy = jest.spyOn(leaveService, 'calculateDays');
  leaveService.validateRequest(mockRequest);
  expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledWith(mockRequest.startDate, mockRequest.endDate);
  // Breaks if we refactor to use a different calculation method!
});

// ✅ GOOD: Tests behavior (input → output)
it('rejects leave requests exceeding balance', () => {
  const result = leaveService.validateRequest({
    startDate: new Date('2025-06-01'),
    endDate: new Date('2025-06-15'),
    balance: 5
  });
  expect(result.valid).toBe(false);
  expect(result.errors).toContain('Insufficient balance');
  // Works regardless of internal implementation!
});

Mistake 2: Not Cleaning Up Between Tests

Tests share state — one test modifies a global/module variable that affects the next test. Tests pass individually but fail when run together.

// ❌ BAD: Shared mutable state
let counter = 0;

it('increments counter', () => {
  counter++;
  expect(counter).toBe(1); // passes
});

it('starts at zero', () => {
  expect(counter).toBe(0); // FAILS — counter is 1 from previous test!
});

// ✅ GOOD: Reset state in beforeEach
let counter;
beforeEach(() => {
  counter = 0;
});

it('increments counter', () => {
  counter++;
  expect(counter).toBe(1);
});

it('starts at zero', () => {
  expect(counter).toBe(0); // passes!
});

Mistake 3: Writing Tests That Are Too Broad

One test case validates 10 things. When it fails, you don't know which assertion broke.

// ❌ BAD: One giant test
it('handles leave request', () => {
  const result = processLeave(request);
  expect(result.status).toBe('pending');
  expect(result.days).toBe(5);
  expect(result.balance).toBe(15);
  expect(result.notifications).toHaveLength(2);
  expect(result.auditLog).toBeDefined();
  // Which one failed? Hard to tell in CI output!
});

// ✅ GOOD: Focused tests with descriptive names
it('sets status to pending for new requests', () => {
  expect(processLeave(request).status).toBe('pending');
});

it('calculates 5 business days for Mon-Fri', () => {
  expect(processLeave(request).days).toBe(5);
});

it('deducts days from employee balance', () => {
  expect(processLeave(request).balance).toBe(15);
});

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, early unit tests checked if axios.get was called with the right URL — tightly coupled to the HTTP library. When migrating from axios to fetch, all tests broke despite the feature working correctly. Rewriting tests to check the returned data instead of the HTTP library made them refactor-proof.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Mocking everything, including the module you're testing. If you mock the function under test, you're testing the mock, not your code.

Mitigation: Only mock external dependencies (database, APIs, file system). Never mock the function you're actually testing.

Top 3 Mocking Strategy Mistakes

Mistake 1: Over-Mocking — Mocking Everything Including Internal Modules

When you mock too much, your tests verify the mocks work, not your actual code.

// ❌ BAD: Mocking the module under test
jest.mock('./leaveCalculator');
import { calculateDays } from './leaveCalculator';

it('calculates days', () => {
  calculateDays.mockReturnValue(5);
  expect(calculateDays('2025-03-01', '2025-03-05')).toBe(5);
  // This tests the MOCK, not your code! Useless!
});

// ✅ GOOD: Only mock external dependencies
import { calculateDays } from './leaveCalculator'; // real implementation
jest.mock('@prisma/client'); // mock the database

it('calculates business days correctly', () => {
  const result = calculateDays('2025-03-03', '2025-03-07');
  expect(result).toBe(5); // tests REAL logic
});

Mistake 2: Not Resetting Mocks Between Tests

Mock state leaks between tests, causing unpredictable failures.

// ❌ BAD: Mock state accumulates
const mockSend = jest.fn();

it('sends notification on approval', async () => {
  await approveLeave(1);
  expect(mockSend).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1); // passes
});

it('sends notification on rejection', async () => {
  await rejectLeave(1);
  expect(mockSend).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1); // FAILS — called 2 times total!
});

// ✅ GOOD: Reset mocks
afterEach(() => {
  jest.clearAllMocks(); // or jest.resetAllMocks()
});

Mistake 3: Using Manual Mocks Instead of Dependency Injection

Manually mocking modules with jest.mock() creates fragile tests tied to file paths.

// ❌ BAD: jest.mock with file path (breaks on rename)
jest.mock('../../services/notificationService');

// ✅ GOOD: Dependency injection
class LeaveService {
  constructor(private notifier: INotifier) {}
  
  async approve(id: number) {
    await this.notifier.send('Leave approved');
  }
}

// In tests: inject a mock
const mockNotifier = { send: jest.fn() };
const service = new LeaveService(mockNotifier);
await service.approve(1);
expect(mockNotifier.send).toHaveBeenCalledWith('Leave approved');

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the leave approval service depends on Prisma (database), Socket.io (notifications), and a mailer. Tests inject mock implementations for all three, verifying that approval logic calls the right methods without needing a real database or SMTP server.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using jest.mock() at the top of the file — it applies to ALL tests in that file, even ones that don't need it.

Mitigation: Use jest.spyOn() inside individual tests for granular control. Or use manual mock injection via constructor parameters. This gives each test full control over its own mocks.

Definition

A Mocking Strategy defines when and how to replace real dependencies (databases, APIs, file systems) with controlled substitutes in tests. Mocks let you test your code in isolation — verifying behavior without needing a real PostgreSQL database, SMTP server, or third-party API.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval flow touches 4 dependencies: Prisma (database), Socket.io (real-time notifications), email service, and an audit logger. Without mocking, each test would need a running database, Socket.io server, and email server. Mocking these dependencies lets the test suite run in 3 seconds instead of 30+ seconds.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Mocking strategy for leave approval tests
import { LeaveService } from './leaveService';

// Mock dependencies
const mockPrisma = {
  leave: {
    findUnique: jest.fn(),
    update: jest.fn()
  },
  employee: {
    update: jest.fn()
  },
  $transaction: jest.fn(fn => fn(mockPrisma))
};

const mockNotifier = {
  send: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue(true)
};

const mockAuditLog = {
  log: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue(true)
};

describe('LeaveService.approve', () => {
  let service: LeaveService;

  beforeEach(() => {
    jest.clearAllMocks();
    service = new LeaveService(mockPrisma, mockNotifier, mockAuditLog);
  });

  it('updates leave status and deducts balance', async () => {
    // Stub: return predefined data
    mockPrisma.leave.findUnique.mockResolvedValue({
      id: 1, status: 'pending', employeeId: 42, days: 3
    });
    mockPrisma.leave.update.mockResolvedValue({ id: 1, status: 'approved' });

    await service.approve(1, { approverId: 10 });

    // Mock: verify correct calls
    expect(mockPrisma.leave.update).toHaveBeenCalledWith({
      where: { id: 1 },
      data: expect.objectContaining({ status: 'approved' })
    });
    expect(mockNotifier.send).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
    expect(mockAuditLog.log).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
      expect.objectContaining({ action: 'LEAVE_APPROVED' })
    );
  });
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Mocks returning data that doesn't match the real implementation's shape. Your test passes with { id: 1, name: 'Test' } but real Prisma returns { id: 1, name: 'Test', createdAt: Date, _count: {} }.

Mitigation: Use TypeScript to type your mocks. Create mock factories that return the full shape of real data. Or use jest-mock-extended for type-safe auto-mocking.

Top 3 Integration Testing Mistakes

Mistake 1: Not Isolating the Test Database

Running integration tests against the development database — tests modify real data, and dev changes make tests flaky.

// ❌ BAD: Tests use dev database
// .env
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/hr_dev

// ✅ GOOD: Separate test database with cleanup
// .env.test
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/hr_test

// jest.setup.ts
beforeAll(async () => {
  await prisma.$executeRaw`TRUNCATE TABLE leaves, employees CASCADE`;
  // Seed test data
  await prisma.employee.create({ data: testEmployee });
});

afterAll(async () => {
  await prisma.$disconnect();
});

Mistake 2: Testing Through the UI Instead of the API

Using E2E tools (Playwright/Cypress) for what should be API integration tests. UI tests are 10× slower and more fragile.

// ❌ BAD: Testing API logic through browser
await page.goto('/leaves');
await page.click('button[data-testid="create-leave"]');
await page.fill('#startDate', '2025-06-01');
await page.click('button[type="submit"]');
// Takes 5+ seconds, depends on frontend rendering

// ✅ GOOD: Test API directly with supertest
const response = await request(app)
  .post('/api/leaves')
  .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
  .send({ startDate: '2025-06-01', endDate: '2025-06-05', type: 'casual' });

expect(response.status).toBe(201);
expect(response.body.status).toBe('pending');
// Takes 50ms, no browser needed

Mistake 3: Not Testing Error Paths

Only testing the happy path. Integration tests should verify error responses, validation failures, and edge cases.

// ❌ BAD: Only happy path
it('creates a leave', async () => {
  const res = await request(app).post('/api/leaves').send(validData);
  expect(res.status).toBe(201);
});

// ✅ GOOD: Test error paths too
it('returns 400 for backdated leave', async () => {
  const res = await request(app).post('/api/leaves').send({
    startDate: '2024-01-01', endDate: '2024-01-05'
  });
  expect(res.status).toBe(400);
  expect(res.body.error).toContain('Cannot backdate');
});

it('returns 401 without auth token', async () => {
  const res = await request(app).post('/api/leaves').send(validData);
  expect(res.status).toBe(401);
});

it('returns 409 for overlapping leave', async () => {
  await request(app).post('/api/leaves').send(validData); // first
  const res = await request(app).post('/api/leaves').send(validData); // duplicate
  expect(res.status).toBe(409);
});

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, integration tests verify the full Fastify → service → Prisma → PostgreSQL flow. Each test uses a transaction that rolls back after the test, keeping the database clean without needing to truncate tables between tests.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Integration tests that depend on execution order. Test B passes only if Test A runs first (because A creates data B needs).

Mitigation: Each test should set up its own data in beforeEach and be independent. Use factories to create fresh test data per test.

Definition

Integration Testing verifies that multiple components work together correctly — your API route + service layer + database, or your React component + API client + state management. Unlike unit tests (one function in isolation), integration tests exercise the real interactions between layers.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, an integration test verifies the complete leave approval flow: HTTP request → Fastify route → LeaveService → Prisma → PostgreSQL → Socket.io notification. It catches bugs that unit tests miss — like a Prisma query that's valid TypeScript but produces wrong SQL, or a route handler that doesn't pass the right data to the service.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Integration test: Leave creation API
import { buildApp } from '../app';
import { prisma } from '../db';

describe('POST /api/leaves', () => {
  let app;
  let authToken;

  beforeAll(async () => {
    app = await buildApp(); // real Fastify app
    // Create test employee and get JWT
    const emp = await prisma.employee.create({
      data: { name: 'Test User', email: 'test@hr.com', leaveBalance: 20 }
    });
    authToken = generateJWT({ id: emp.id, role: 'employee' });
  });

  afterAll(async () => {
    await prisma.leave.deleteMany();
    await prisma.employee.deleteMany();
    await prisma.$disconnect();
    await app.close();
  });

  it('creates leave and deducts balance', async () => {
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${authToken}` },
      payload: {
        startDate: '2025-06-02', // Monday
        endDate: '2025-06-06',   // Friday
        type: 'casual',
        reason: 'Family event'
      }
    });

    expect(response.statusCode).toBe(201);
    const body = JSON.parse(response.body);
    expect(body.status).toBe('pending');
    expect(body.days).toBe(5);

    // Verify database state
    const emp = await prisma.employee.findFirst();
    expect(emp.leaveBalance).toBe(15); // 20 - 5
  });

  it('rejects overlapping leave requests', async () => {
    // First leave already created in previous test
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${authToken}` },
      payload: {
        startDate: '2025-06-04', // overlaps with existing
        endDate: '2025-06-10',
        type: 'casual'
      }
    });

    expect(response.statusCode).toBe(409);
  });
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Integration tests that are slow because they start/stop the server and database for each test file. A 200-test suite takes 5+ minutes.

Mitigation: Start the server once in globalSetup, share the connection across tests, and use transactions or TRUNCATE for data isolation. Fastify's app.inject() avoids real HTTP overhead.

Definition

Playwright E2E testing automates real browser interactions — clicking buttons, filling forms, navigating pages — to verify that the entire application works from the user's perspective. Unlike unit tests that check individual functions, Playwright tests the full stack: frontend → API → database → response rendering.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React 18), 60+ Playwright E2E tests cover the critical user journeys: creating a contact, editing details, uploading a CSV with 500 contacts, cropping an avatar with Canvas API, and deleting contacts. These tests run in CI on every push, catching regressions that unit tests miss — like a form submission that works in isolation but fails when the router state is wrong.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// ContactsHub: Playwright E2E tests
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test.describe('Contact Management', () => {
  test.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => {
    await page.goto('/contacts');
  });

  test('creates a new contact', async ({ page }) => {
    await page.click('[data-testid="add-contact"]');
    await page.fill('[name="name"]', 'John Doe');
    await page.fill('[name="email"]', 'john@example.com');
    await page.fill('[name="phone"]', '+91 9876543210');
    await page.click('[type="submit"]');

    // Verify success toast
    await expect(page.locator('.toast-success')).toBeVisible();
    // Verify contact appears in list
    await expect(page.locator('text=John Doe')).toBeVisible();
  });

  test('uploads CSV with 500 contacts', async ({ page }) => {
    const fileChooser = page.waitForEvent('filechooser');
    await page.click('[data-testid="csv-upload"]');
    const chooser = await fileChooser;
    await chooser.setFiles('test-data/500-contacts.csv');

    // Wait for bulk upload progress
    await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="upload-progress"]'))
      .toContainText('500/500');
    await expect(page.locator('.toast-success'))
      .toContainText('500 contacts imported');
  });

  test('handles duplicate email during creation', async ({ page }) => {
    // Create first contact
    await page.click('[data-testid="add-contact"]');
    await page.fill('[name="email"]', 'duplicate@test.com');
    await page.click('[type="submit"]');

    // Try to create duplicate
    await page.click('[data-testid="add-contact"]');
    await page.fill('[name="email"]', 'duplicate@test.com');
    await page.click('[type="submit"]');

    await expect(page.locator('.error-message'))
      .toContainText('Email already exists');
  });
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Hardcoding waitForTimeout(3000) to wait for elements. This makes tests slow and still flaky — sometimes 3 seconds isn't enough, sometimes it's too much.

Mitigation: Use Playwright's auto-waiting: await expect(locator).toBeVisible() automatically waits up to the timeout. Use waitForResponse to wait for specific API calls to complete.

Definition

Playwright E2E solves the "works on my machine" deployment problem. Code can pass all unit and integration tests but still fail in the browser due to CSS issues, JavaScript loading order, race conditions in the UI, or API integration bugs. E2E tests catch these by testing what the user actually sees and does.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, unit tests for the Canvas API image cropper passed perfectly — the crop function returned the correct pixel data. But in production, the avatar upload broke on Safari because the Canvas API returned a different blob format. A Playwright E2E test running on WebKit (Safari engine) would have caught this before deployment.

Problems Solved

  • Cross-browser bugs: Test on Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit in CI
  • Full-stack integration: Verify frontend → API → database → UI update cycle
  • User journey validation: Ensure multi-step workflows (create → edit → delete) work end-to-end
  • Visual regressions: Screenshot comparison catches CSS layout breaks

Code Example

// playwright.config.ts
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './e2e',
  retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0,
  use: {
    baseURL: 'http://localhost:3000',
    trace: 'on-first-retry', // capture trace on failures
    screenshot: 'only-on-failure'
  },
  projects: [
    { name: 'chromium', use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] } },
    { name: 'firefox', use: { ...devices['Desktop Firefox'] } },
    { name: 'webkit', use: { ...devices['Desktop Safari'] } },
    { name: 'mobile', use: { ...devices['iPhone 13'] } }
  ],
  webServer: {
    command: 'npm run dev',
    port: 3000,
    reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI
  }
});

// ContactsHub: 60+ E2E tests organized by feature
// e2e/contacts/crud.spec.ts — create, read, update, delete
// e2e/contacts/csv-upload.spec.ts — bulk import
// e2e/contacts/avatar.spec.ts — image crop + upload
// e2e/auth/login.spec.ts — authentication flow
// e2e/search/filter.spec.ts — search + filter

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, a Playwright test for CSV upload failed intermittently in CI. The trace viewer showed the upload button was clicked before the file input was ready. Using page.waitForEvent('filechooser') instead of page.click() followed by page.setInputFiles() fixed the race condition — the test waits for the file dialog before proceeding.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Writing too many E2E tests. They're slow (seconds each) and expensive to maintain. 500 E2E tests taking 30 minutes blocks CI.

Mitigation: Follow the testing pyramid: many unit tests, some integration tests, few E2E tests. E2E tests should cover critical user journeys (login, purchase, data upload), not individual component behavior.
